name: Cadence Builder
description: Train your legs to turn over faster to reduce overstriding and improve efficiency.
instructions: Run at an easy pace while counting your steps for 30 seconds. Multiply by 2 to get steps per minute. Aim to increase this by 5–10% using a metronome app set to your target cadence. Run 4 × 2 minutes at target cadence with 1 minute easy between each.

name: Short Stride Run
description: A focused drill to eliminate overstriding by practising landing with your foot beneath your hips.
instructions: On a flat surface, run at a comfortable pace while consciously shortening each stride. Imagine your feet landing directly under your centre of mass, not in front of your body. Run 3 × 5 minutes with full recovery between sets. Focus on the feeling — not speed.

name: Arm Drive Drill
description: Isolate and correct arm mechanics to eliminate arm crossing and improve forward propulsion.
instructions: Stand in front of a mirror. Drive your arms back and forth parallel to your body — elbows bent at 90°, hands relaxed (imagine holding crisps without crushing them). Arms should swing front-to-back, not across your body. Practise for 60 seconds standing, then carry this into a 10-minute easy run. Check in every 2 minutes.

name: 90-Degree Elbow Run
description: Reinforce correct elbow angle to improve arm drive efficiency.
instructions: Before your next run, set an alarm for every 3 minutes. Each time it goes off, check: are your elbows at roughly 90°? Are your hands crossing your body's centre line? Correct if needed. Run 20–30 minutes total. Over time this becomes automatic.

name: Wall Lean Drill
description: Teaches the correct forward lean from the ankles — not the waist.
instructions: Stand facing a wall, arms out, fingertips touching it. Lean your entire body forward as one unit from your ankles (like a falling plank). Hold for 5 seconds. Reset. Repeat 10 times. This is the feeling to replicate when running. Follow with a 10-minute run, focusing on leaning from ankles.

name: Run Tall Drill
description: Corrects hunched posture and excessive forward lean from the waist.
instructions: Before running, stand tall: imagine a string pulling you up from the crown of your head. Squeeze your glutes gently. Roll your shoulders back and down. Hold this for 10 seconds. Now start running, maintaining this tall feeling. Every 5 minutes, do a mental posture check: am I collapsing forward at the waist?

name: Gaze Focus Drill
description: Trains you to keep your head level and gaze forward rather than down.
instructions: Pick a landmark 10–15 metres ahead of you on your next run (a lamppost, a crack in the pavement). Keep your gaze on it as you run. When you pass it, pick the next one. Your head should feel level — not tucked down toward your chest. Practice this for the first 5–10 minutes of your next 3 runs.

name: A-Skip Drill
description: Classic track drill that teaches correct foot strike position and knee drive.
instructions: Walk forward. On each step, drive one knee up to hip height while rising onto the ball of your standing foot. Place your foot back down beneath your hip — not in front of it. Progress to a marching pace then a skipping rhythm. 3 × 20 metres. Focus on the foot landing under the hip each time.

name: Barefoot Grass Strides
description: Running briefly barefoot on grass naturally encourages a midfoot strike pattern.
instructions: Find a clean, flat grassy area. Remove shoes and socks. Run 4–6 × 60-metre strides at a comfortable pace. The ground feedback will naturally encourage your foot to land underneath your body rather than heel-striking out in front. Walk back between each stride. Cool down with shoes on.

name: Single-Leg Balance
description: Builds the hip stability needed to prevent hip drop during the single-leg stance phase of running.
instructions: Stand on one leg. Hold for 30 seconds without letting your pelvis drop to the opposite side. To progress: close your eyes, or stand on a folded towel for an unstable surface. 3 × 30 seconds each leg. When you can do this easily, add small knee bends (mini single-leg squats) while keeping your pelvis level.

name: Glute Bridge
description: Strengthens the glutes and hip abductors to reduce hip drop and improve pelvic stability while running.
instructions: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat on the floor hip-width apart. Push through your heels and lift your hips until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees. Squeeze your glutes at the top. Hold 2 seconds. Lower slowly. 3 × 15 reps. Progress to single-leg glute bridges when ready.

name: Low Horizontal Bounds
description: Trains forward propulsion rather than upward bounce, reducing vertical oscillation.
instructions: From a jog, push off with one foot and drive forward — not upward. Think of skimming the ground like a stone. Land softly on the ball of the opposite foot and immediately push forward again. 4 × 30 metres. The goal is distance per bound, not height. Focus on feeling like you are running along a low ceiling.

name: High Knees
description: Improves knee drive, hip flexor activation, and overall leg turnover.
instructions: Run in place (or slowly forward), driving each knee up toward hip height alternately. Keep your torso upright and tall. Arms drive back and forth at 90°. Land softly on the ball of each foot. 3 × 30 seconds with 30 seconds rest. Focus on staying light and quick rather than lifting as high as possible.

name: Butt Kicks
description: Improves heel recovery and reinforces the quick, compact leg cycle needed for efficient running.
instructions: Jog slowly, flicking each heel up toward your glutes as quickly as possible after toe-off. Keep your thighs perpendicular to the ground — resist swinging them forward. Stay on your toes. 3 × 30 seconds. Think of your heels as being on a spring — quick and light.

name: Shoulder Drop Check
description: Reduces tension in the shoulders and upper body that leads to arm crossing and energy waste.
instructions: Every 5 minutes during your next run, do a tension check: are your shoulders creeping up toward your ears? Deliberately drop them down and back. Shake out your hands as if you are trying to flick water off your fingertips — this releases grip tension throughout the arm. Make this a habit on every run.
